entry.title: Re: Concern about "Inscriptions"
entry.summary:
The discussion highlights a significant challenge in regulating data on blockchains, specifically concerning the use of public keys to encode non-standard or arbitrary data. When attempts are made to restrict certain types of data, such as "arbitrary data," there's an innovative workaround that involves encoding this restricted data within public key data. Public keys, which typically appear random, can thus serve as a covert channel for data transmission, making it difficult to discern whether they are genuine cryptographic keys or carriers of encoded information.
This technique mirrors strategies used to bypass internet censorship, where protocols might be tunneled through seemingly innocuous connections like HTTPS, exploiting their indistinguishable nature from regular data after initial handshakes. Such methods have been observed in various contexts, including Tor bridge nodes, which utilize HTTPS streams to secretly carry data. This approach effectively camouflages the transmission, leveraging the inherent randomness of HTTPS post-handshake data flows and public keys alike.
Furthermore, the practice is not merely theoretical; it has been previously implemented by entities such as Counterparty, who have encoded data within public key frameworks. This historical precedent underscores the practical reality and potential recurrence of such strategies if stringent data regulations are imposed without accommodating the complex nature of cryptographic systems. Hence, any regulatory measures must carefully consider these dynamics to avoid unintended loopholes that could compromise the effectiveness of the regulations.
